primary_concept: Incremental app development — starting from a no-persistence MVP and evolving the same app across three modules to add persistence and then AI-powered features
secondary_concepts: Writing a structured PRD before prompting
Scoping an MVP vs. full feature set
Phase-based building to avoid context rot
Debugging at the cosmetic, functional, and catastrophic levels
JSON file persistence for portable data
Integrating an LLM API to generate flashcard content
API key security and cost guardrails
mvp_features: Add a flashcard with a front (question) and back (answer)
Flip a card to reveal the answer
Navigate forward and backward through the card deck
Delete a card from the deck
Display total card count and current position in the deck
stretch_features: Persist cards using localStorage so the deck survives a page refresh (Module 2)
Save and load cards from a JSON file for portability across devices (Module 2)
Connect to a Supabase database to enable multi-device access (Module 2)
Generate flashcard content automatically using an LLM API (Module 3)
Allow the learner to specify a topic and have the LLM produce a ready-made deck (Module 3)
Track which cards have been marked as known vs. still learning (Module 3 stretch)
forbidden_features: User accounts or login — requires authentication infrastructure beyond course scope
Sharing decks with other users — requires user identity and a multi-user database
Real-time sync across devices — requires a backend server beyond the course scope
Payment processing or premium deck purchases — explicitly flagged in Module 3 conclusion as beyond ceiling
Spaced repetition algorithm — complex stateful logic that obscures the persistence and API concepts being taught
Any external API in Module 1 — APIs are not introduced until Module 3
Any persistence mechanism in Module 1 — persistence is not introduced until Module 2
learner_outcome: Learners will be able to plan, build, and iteratively extend the Flashcards app from a zero-persistence MVP through adding localStorage and database persistence to integrating an LLM API that auto-generates card content, demonstrating a complete app development lifecycle.
instructor_talking_points: In Module 1, show how writing a PRD first produces a cleaner initial prompt — contrast a vague prompt ('make me a flashcard app') with the structured version derived from the PRD.
When adding localStorage in Module 2, narrate the moment of refresh: show cards disappearing without persistence, then reappearing after — make the problem visceral before showing the fix.
When switching from localStorage to a JSON file in Module 2, explain the trade-off out loud: localStorage is tied to one browser on one device, while a JSON file travels with the app.
In Module 3, clearly distinguish between 'using an LLM to build the app' and 'using an LLM as a feature inside the app' — the LLM API call that generates flashcard content is the latter.
scope_warnings: Adding a score or progress tracker that remembers history across sessions — this requires persistence and should not appear until Module 2.
Suggesting a spaced repetition system (like Anki) — the algorithm requires complex state management that will overwhelm both the learner and the coding agent in early phases.
Prompting the coding agent to 'make it shareable' — sharing requires user accounts, a multi-user database, and URL routing, which are all out of scope for this course.
Allowing the LLM to generate cards without any guardrails — in Module 3 this is the right moment to discuss prompt injection and API cost limits before wiring up the feature.
Asking for a leaderboard or competitive mode — leaderboards require user identity and a persistent score database, pulling in Module 2 and authentication concepts simultaneously.
Requesting an image or audio side to each card — binary media storage requires infrastructure (e.g., object storage buckets) not covered in any module.
